title: Socket RAPL Feature Validation at SOC Integration Level
subsection content: This test plan targets the post-silicon validation of Socket RAPL functionality at the highest feature hierarchy, focusing on its integration and operation within the SOC. Socket RAPL provides power and frequency limit enforcement across multiple domains (core, memory, etc.), interfacing with BIOS, TPMI, and various IPs. Key flows include BIOS initialization and locking of RAPL limits, TPMI-based domain enumeration and access, propagation of power/frequency ceilings from root to leaf IMHs and CBB dies, aggregation and real-time reporting of performance status, and coordinated enforcement via cross-domain PID controllers. The plan covers interactions between BIOS, TPMI, IMHs, CBB dies, and system-level status reporting, ensuring robust enforcement and accurate system state reflection under all operational and stress scenarios.

title: Socket RAPL Feature Validation at SOC Integration Level
subsection content: The scope encompasses validation of BIOS and TPMI configuration integrity (including programming, locking, and domain enumeration), propagation and enforcement of power/frequency limits across all IPs, aggregation and reporting of LEAF_PERF_STATUS and RAPL_PERF_STATUS, stress and boundary testing (including negative scenarios and error handling), and cross-domain PID controller interactions. All flows from initialization to runtime enforcement, status reporting, and system response to invalid or extreme configurations are included. Out-of-scope items are any features not directly related to Socket RAPL, such as non-RAPL power management, unrelated BIOS/TPMI features, or IPs outside the RAPL domain.

title: Socket RAPL Feature Validation at SOC Integration Level
Socket RAPL Feature Validation at SOC Integration Level
subsection content: All features require access to BIOS configuration interfaces, TPMI registers, CSR access for RAPL limits and lock bits, and system-level monitoring tools for status registers. Hardware must support domain enumeration and real-time status reporting. Stress tools for generating extreme power/frequency demands and error injection capabilities are needed.

title: Socket RAPL Feature Validation at SOC Integration Level
subsection content: Potential security threats include unauthorized modification of RAPL limits post-lock, improper domain enumeration exposing sensitive configuration, and manipulation of status reporting. Mitigations involve strict enforcement of lock bits, access control on TPMI and CSR interfaces, and validation of error handling for invalid configuration attempts.

title: Socket RAPL Feature Validation at SOC Integration Level
subsection content: Risks include incomplete coverage of cross-domain interactions, undetected failures in lock enforcement, and inaccurate status aggregation under stress. Contingencies involve additional targeted testing for edge cases, enhanced monitoring during stress scenarios, and fallback to manual validation if automated tools fail.

title: Socket RAPL Feature Validation at SOC Integration Level
subsection content: Prerequisites include a fully functional BIOS with RAPL programming capability, TPMI register access, SOC hardware supporting all RAPL domains, and availability of stress and error injection tools. Initial domain mapping and lock bit configuration must be completed prior to test execution.

title: Stress, Boundary & Negative Scenarios
subsection content: Apply extreme power/frequency demands, disable limits, and attempt invalid configurations (e.g., locked register modification, turbo disable). Observe system-level error handling, throttling, and status updates. Expected outcome: system remains stable, errors are handled gracefully, and limits are enforced under all conditions.

title: Stress Test: Extreme Power and Frequency Demand with RAPL Limits Disabled
subsection content: This test validates system behavior when RAPL power limits are maximized or disabled and FastRAPL is turned off under extreme workload conditions.

title: Stress Test: Extreme Power and Frequency Demand with RAPL Limits Disabled
subsection content: Configure PL1 and PL2 to maximum allowed values, disable FastRAPL, apply maximum synthetic workload, and observe system response and throttling mechanisms.

title: Stress Test: Extreme Power and Frequency Demand with RAPL Limits Disabled
subsection content: No explicit randomization; test uses maximum allowed values for PL1/PL2 and disables FastRAPL.

title: Stress Test: Extreme Power and Frequency Demand with RAPL Limits Disabled
subsection content: Requires BIOS/CSR interface unlocked, access to CSR/TPMI, mailbox command for FastRAPL, and synthetic workload generator.

title: Stress Test: Extreme Power and Frequency Demand with RAPL Limits Disabled
subsection content: Involves RAPL, FastRAPL, PROCHOT/PMAX hardware throttling, IMON, and RAPL status registers.

title: Stress Test: Extreme Power and Frequency Demand with RAPL Limits Disabled
subsection content: Observability via IMON, RAPL energy status, RAPL_perf_status, and IO_THERM_INDICATIONS.FAST_RAPL_THROTTLE[22].

title: Maximize RAPL Limits and Disable FastRAPL Under Extreme Workload
subsection content: Boot the system with BIOS/CSR interface unlocked. Set Socket RAPL PL1 and PL2 limits to their maximum allowed values using CSR or TPMI. Disable FastRAPL by issuing the WRITE_PCU_MISC_CONFIG mailbox command. Apply a synthetic workload that drives all cores and uncore to 100% utilization and maximizes memory bandwidth. Monitor IMON, RAPL energy status, RAPL_perf_status, and IO_THERM_INDICATIONS.FAST_RAPL_THROTTLE[22] throughout the test. Observe system power consumption and any hardware throttling events.

title: Maximize RAPL Limits and Disable FastRAPL Under Extreme Workload
subsection content: 1. Boot system with BIOS/CSR interface unlocked. 2. Set PL1 and PL2 to maximum allowed values via CSR or TPMI. 3. Disable FastRAPL using WRITE_PCU_MISC_CONFIG mailbox command. 4. Launch synthetic workload to maximize core and uncore activity. 5. Continuously monitor IMON, RAPL energy status, RAPL_perf_status, and IO_THERM_INDICATIONS.FAST_RAPL_THROTTLE[22]. 6. Observe for any assertion of PROCHOT/PMAX or other hardware throttling. 7. After sustained workload, stop the test and collect logs.

title: Maximize RAPL Limits and Disable FastRAPL Under Extreme Workload
subsection content: Pass if system power consumption does not exceed platform physical constraints (TDP, VR, PSU), hardware throttling (PROCHOT/PMAX) is asserted if needed, and no system instability or unhandled errors occur. Fail if system exceeds physical constraints, does not throttle, or experiences instability.

title: Maximize RAPL Limits and Disable FastRAPL Under Extreme Workload
subsection content: Confirm via IMON, RAPL energy status, RAPL_perf_status, and IO_THERM_INDICATIONS.FAST_RAPL_THROTTLE[22] logs that throttling occurs as expected and no errors are present.

title: Maximize RAPL Limits and Disable FastRAPL Under Extreme Workload
subsection content: Enable SST, Central, and Concurrency checkers. Enable PVT checkers for power and thermal events.

title: Maximize RAPL Limits and Disable FastRAPL Under Extreme Workload
subsection content: Tool and Content Requirements: Use OSBV/SVOS with supported version, PMON for power monitoring, PythonSV for register access, and synthetic workload generator for stress.

title: Maximize RAPL Limits and Disable FastRAPL Under Extreme Workload
subsection content: Test Requirements: BIOS with unlocked CSR/TPMI, default OS kernel and scheduler, all relevant drivers loaded, and no power management overrides.

title: Maximize RAPL Limits and Disable FastRAPL Under Extreme Workload
subsection title: Test HW Requirements and HW Configuration *
subsection content: Platform with supported SoC stepping, full DIMM population, PCIe devices as required, VR and PSU instrumentation for power measurement, and thermal solution capable of handling maximum TDP.

title: Disable PL2 and FastRAPL, Observe Hardware Throttling
subsection content: With system running at maximum workload, dynamically disable PL2 and FastRAPL via CSR/TPMI and mailbox command. Continue to apply the synthetic workload and monitor for hardware-level throttling via PROCHOT/PMAX and IO_THERM_INDICATIONS.FAST_RAPL_THROTTLE[22]. Collect IMON and RAPL status data to verify system response.

title: Disable PL2 and FastRAPL, Observe Hardware Throttling
subsection content: 1. Boot system and configure PL1/PL2 to maximum. 2. Start synthetic workload. 3. Disable PL2 via CSR/TPMI. 4. Disable FastRAPL via mailbox command. 5. Monitor IMON, RAPL energy status, RAPL_perf_status, and IO_THERM_INDICATIONS.FAST_RAPL_THROTTLE[22]. 6. Check for assertion of PROCHOT/PMAX. 7. End test and collect all logs.

title: Disable PL2 and FastRAPL, Observe Hardware Throttling
subsection content: Pass if system throttles via hardware mechanisms when limits are disabled and does not exceed platform constraints. Fail if system does not throttle or experiences instability.

title: Disable PL2 and FastRAPL, Observe Hardware Throttling
subsection content: Use IMON, RAPL energy status, and IO_THERM_INDICATIONS.FAST_RAPL_THROTTLE[22] to confirm hardware throttling is engaged.

title: Disable PL2 and FastRAPL, Observe Hardware Throttling
subsection content: Enable SST, Central, and Concurrency checkers. PVT checkers for thermal and power events.

title: Disable PL2 and FastRAPL, Observe Hardware Throttling
subsection content: Tool and Content Requirements: OSBV/SVOS, PMON, PythonSV, synthetic workload generator.

title: Disable PL2 and FastRAPL, Observe Hardware Throttling
subsection content: Test Requirements: BIOS with unlocked CSR/TPMI, default OS and drivers.

title: Disable PL2 and FastRAPL, Observe Hardware Throttling
subsection title: Test HW Requirements and HW Configuration *
subsection content: Platform with full power and thermal instrumentation, SoC at supported stepping, and maximum TDP thermal solution.

title: Monitor RAPL_perf_status and Performance Degradation During Throttling
subsection content: During sustained maximum workload with RAPL limits disabled, monitor RAPL_perf_status to track any performance degradation due to hardware throttling. Correlate RAPL_perf_status increments with IMON and throttle signal assertions.

title: Monitor RAPL_perf_status and Performance Degradation During Throttling
subsection content: 1. Boot system and configure as in previous TCs. 2. Apply maximum workload. 3. Disable PL2 and FastRAPL. 4. Continuously read RAPL_perf_status and IMON. 5. Log any assertion of IO_THERM_INDICATIONS.FAST_RAPL_THROTTLE[22], PROCHOT, or PMAX. 6. After test, analyze RAPL_perf_status increments and correlate with throttle events.

title: Monitor RAPL_perf_status and Performance Degradation During Throttling
subsection content: Pass if RAPL_perf_status reflects performance degradation only when hardware throttling is asserted and system remains stable. Fail if RAPL_perf_status does not increment during throttling or system is unstable.

title: Monitor RAPL_perf_status and Performance Degradation During Throttling
subsection content: Use RAPL_perf_status logs and throttle signal monitoring to confirm correct performance reporting.

title: Monitor RAPL_perf_status and Performance Degradation During Throttling
subsection content: Enable SST, Central, and Concurrency checkers. PVT checkers for performance and power.

title: Monitor RAPL_perf_status and Performance Degradation During Throttling
subsection content: Tool and Content Requirements: OSBV/SVOS, PMON, PythonSV, synthetic workload generator.

title: Monitor RAPL_perf_status and Performance Degradation During Throttling
subsection content: Test Requirements: BIOS with unlocked CSR/TPMI, default OS and drivers.

title: Monitor RAPL_perf_status and Performance Degradation During Throttling
subsection title: Test HW Requirements and HW Configuration *
subsection content: Platform with full power and thermal instrumentation, SoC at supported stepping, and maximum TDP thermal solution.

title: Boundary and Negative Test: Locked Register Modification Attempt
subsection content: This test validates that power limit registers cannot be modified when the lock bit is set, ensuring platform power integrity.

title: Boundary and Negative Test: Locked Register Modification Attempt
subsection content: Lock the relevant CSR or TPMI register, attempt to modify power limits via in-band agent, and verify no changes occur.

title: Boundary and Negative Test: Locked Register Modification Attempt
subsection content: No randomization; test uses fixed lock and modification attempt.

title: Boundary and Negative Test: Locked Register Modification Attempt
subsection content: Requires BIOS/CSR interface with lock capability, in-band agent for register access, and monitoring tools for RAPL_perf_status and power consumption.

title: Boundary and Negative Test: Locked Register Modification Attempt
subsection content: Involves BIOS/CSR, TPMI, RAPL, and in-band software agents.

title: Boundary and Negative Test: Locked Register Modification Attempt
subsection content: Observability via register readback, RAPL_perf_status, and power monitoring.

title: Attempt Register Modification After Lock Bit Set
subsection content: Set the lock bit in the relevant CSR or TPMI register to lock power limit fields. Attempt to modify PL1/PL2 via in-band agent (OS driver or user application). Read back power limit and lock bit fields to verify no change. Monitor RAPL_perf_status and system power consumption to confirm no impact.

title: Attempt Register Modification After Lock Bit Set
subsection content: 1. Boot system and set lock bit in CSR or TPMI register. 2. Attempt to write new value to PL1/PL2 via in-band agent. 3. Read back power limit and lock bit fields. 4. Monitor RAPL_perf_status and system power consumption. 5. Collect logs for analysis.

title: Attempt Register Modification After Lock Bit Set
subsection content: Pass if register write is ignored, power limit and lock bit remain unchanged, RAPL_perf_status does not change, and power consumption is stable. Fail if any field changes or system behavior is affected.

title: Attempt Register Modification After Lock Bit Set
subsection content: Use register readback, RAPL_perf_status, and power monitoring logs to confirm no modification occurred.

title: Attempt Register Modification After Lock Bit Set
subsection content: Enable SST, Central, and Concurrency checkers.

title: Attempt Register Modification After Lock Bit Set
subsection content: Tool and Content Requirements: OSBV/SVOS, PythonSV for register access, PMON for power monitoring.

title: Attempt Register Modification After Lock Bit Set
subsection content: Test Requirements: BIOS with lock capability, default OS and drivers.

title: Attempt Register Modification After Lock Bit Set
subsection title: Test HW Requirements and HW Configuration *
subsection content: Platform with supported SoC, power monitoring instrumentation.

title: Negative Test: Attempt Multiple Writes to Locked Register
subsection content: After setting the lock bit, attempt multiple consecutive writes to the power limit register via different in-band agents. Read back the register after each attempt to verify no changes. Monitor RAPL_perf_status and system power.

title: Negative Test: Attempt Multiple Writes to Locked Register
subsection content: 1. Set lock bit in CSR or TPMI register. 2. Attempt multiple writes to PL1/PL2 via different in-band agents. 3. After each write, read back power limit and lock bit fields. 4. Monitor RAPL_perf_status and power consumption. 5. Collect and analyze logs.

title: Negative Test: Attempt Multiple Writes to Locked Register
subsection content: Pass if all writes are ignored, fields remain unchanged, and system behavior is unaffected. Fail if any write succeeds or system is impacted.

title: Negative Test: Attempt Multiple Writes to Locked Register
subsection content: Confirm via register readback and monitoring tools that no changes occur.

title: Negative Test: Attempt Multiple Writes to Locked Register
subsection content: Enable SST, Central, and Concurrency checkers.

title: Negative Test: Attempt Multiple Writes to Locked Register
subsection content: Tool and Content Requirements: OSBV/SVOS, PythonSV, PMON.

title: Negative Test: Attempt Multiple Writes to Locked Register
subsection content: Test Requirements: BIOS with lock capability, default OS and drivers.

title: Negative Test: Attempt Multiple Writes to Locked Register
subsection title: Test HW Requirements and HW Configuration *
subsection content: Platform with supported SoC, power monitoring instrumentation.

title: Monitor RAPL_perf_status During Locked Register Modification Attempt
subsection content: With lock bit set, attempt to modify power limits and monitor RAPL_perf_status for any unexpected changes. Correlate RAPL_perf_status with attempted writes and system power consumption.

title: Monitor RAPL_perf_status During Locked Register Modification Attempt
subsection content: 1. Set lock bit in CSR or TPMI register. 2. Attempt to modify power limits via in-band agent. 3. Continuously monitor RAPL_perf_status and power consumption during and after attempts. 4. Collect logs for analysis.

title: Monitor RAPL_perf_status During Locked Register Modification Attempt
subsection content: Pass if RAPL_perf_status remains unchanged and system power is stable. Fail if RAPL_perf_status increments or power behavior changes.

title: Monitor RAPL_perf_status During Locked Register Modification Attempt
subsection content: Use RAPL_perf_status and power logs to confirm no impact from modification attempts.

title: Monitor RAPL_perf_status During Locked Register Modification Attempt
subsection content: Enable SST, Central, and Concurrency checkers.

title: Monitor RAPL_perf_status During Locked Register Modification Attempt
subsection content: Tool and Content Requirements: OSBV/SVOS, PythonSV, PMON.

title: Monitor RAPL_perf_status During Locked Register Modification Attempt
subsection content: Test Requirements: BIOS with lock capability, default OS and drivers.

title: Monitor RAPL_perf_status During Locked Register Modification Attempt

subsection content: Platform with supported SoC, power monitoring instrumentation.

title: Negative Test: Attempt to Disable Always-Active PL1 Limit
subsection content: This test validates that attempts to disable the always-active PL1 power limit are ignored and PL1 enforcement remains in effect.

title: Negative Test: Attempt to Disable Always-Active PL1 Limit
subsection content: Attempt to disable PL1 by writing 0 to PWR_LIM_EN field and verify PL1 remains active and system power stays within TDP.

title: Negative Test: Attempt to Disable Always-Active PL1 Limit
subsection content: No randomization; test uses fixed disable attempt.

title: Negative Test: Attempt to Disable Always-Active PL1 Limit
subsection content: Requires access to PL1 control register via CSR, TPMI, or MSR, and monitoring tools for power and RAPL_perf_status.

title: Negative Test: Attempt to Disable Always-Active PL1 Limit
subsection content: Involves RAPL, PL1 enforcement, and system power management.

title: Negative Test: Attempt to Disable Always-Active PL1 Limit
subsection content: Observability via register readback, RAPL_perf_status, and power monitoring.

title: Attempt to Disable PL1 Limit via Supported Interface
subsection content: With system running default RAPL configuration, attempt to disable PL1 by writing 0 to PWR_LIM_EN field in the PL1 control register via CSR, TPMI, or MSR. Read back PWR_LIM_EN to verify PL1 remains enabled. Monitor system power consumption and RAPL_perf_status for any changes.

title: Attempt to Disable PL1 Limit via Supported Interface
subsection content: 1. Boot system with default RAPL configuration. 2. Attempt to write 0 to PWR_LIM_EN field in PL1 control register via supported interface. 3. Read back PWR_LIM_EN field. 4. Monitor system power consumption and RAPL_perf_status. 5. Collect logs for analysis.

title: Attempt to Disable PL1 Limit via Supported Interface
subsection content: Pass if PL1 remains active, PWR_LIM_EN field is unchanged, system power stays within TDP, and RAPL_perf_status reflects PL1 enforcement. Fail if PL1 is disabled or system exceeds TDP.

title: Attempt to Disable PL1 Limit via Supported Interface
subsection content: Use register readback, power monitoring, and RAPL_perf_status logs to confirm PL1 enforcement.

title: Attempt to Disable PL1 Limit via Supported Interface
subsection content: Enable SST, Central, and Concurrency checkers.

title: Attempt to Disable PL1 Limit via Supported Interface
subsection content: Tool and Content Requirements: OSBV/SVOS, PythonSV, PMON.

title: Attempt to Disable PL1 Limit via Supported Interface
subsection content: Test Requirements: BIOS with default RAPL configuration, default OS and drivers.

title: Attempt to Disable PL1 Limit via Supported Interface
subsection title: Test HW Requirements and HW Configuration *
subsection content: Platform with supported SoC, power monitoring instrumentation.

title: Negative Test: Attempt Multiple PL1 Disable Writes
subsection content: Attempt to disable PL1 by writing 0 to PWR_LIM_EN field multiple times via different supported interfaces (CSR, TPMI, MSR). After each attempt, read back PWR_LIM_EN and monitor system power and RAPL_perf_status.

title: Negative Test: Attempt Multiple PL1 Disable Writes
subsection content: 1. Boot system with default RAPL configuration. 2. Attempt to write 0 to PWR_LIM_EN via CSR, TPMI, and MSR sequentially. 3. After each attempt, read back PWR_LIM_EN. 4. Monitor system power and RAPL_perf_status. 5. Collect and analyze logs.

title: Negative Test: Attempt Multiple PL1 Disable Writes
subsection content: Pass if all disable attempts are ignored, PL1 remains active, and system power stays within TDP. Fail if any attempt disables PL1 or system exceeds TDP.

title: Negative Test: Attempt Multiple PL1 Disable Writes
subsection content: Confirm via register readback and monitoring tools that PL1 remains enforced.

title: Negative Test: Attempt Multiple PL1 Disable Writes
subsection content: Enable SST, Central, and Concurrency checkers.

title: Negative Test: Attempt Multiple PL1 Disable Writes
subsection content: Tool and Content Requirements: OSBV/SVOS, PythonSV, PMON.

title: Negative Test: Attempt Multiple PL1 Disable Writes
subsection content: Test Requirements: BIOS with default RAPL configuration, default OS and drivers.

title: Negative Test: Attempt Multiple PL1 Disable Writes

title: Monitor RAPL_perf_status During PL1 Disable Attempt
subsection content: Attempt to disable PL1 and monitor RAPL_perf_status for any changes. Correlate RAPL_perf_status with attempted writes and system power consumption to verify PL1 enforcement.

title: Monitor RAPL_perf_status During PL1 Disable Attempt
subsection content: 1. Boot system with default RAPL configuration. 2. Attempt to disable PL1 via supported interface. 3. Continuously monitor RAPL_perf_status and power consumption during and after attempts. 4. Collect logs for analysis.

title: Monitor RAPL_perf_status During PL1 Disable Attempt
subsection content: Pass if RAPL_perf_status reflects PL1 enforcement and system power remains within TDP. Fail if RAPL_perf_status does not reflect enforcement or system exceeds TDP.

title: Monitor RAPL_perf_status During PL1 Disable Attempt
subsection content: Use RAPL_perf_status and power logs to confirm PL1 enforcement.

title: Monitor RAPL_perf_status During PL1 Disable Attempt

title: Monitor RAPL_perf_status During PL1 Disable Attempt
subsection content: Test Requirements: BIOS with default RAPL configuration, default OS and drivers.

title: Monitor RAPL_perf_status During PL1 Disable Attempt

title: Stress and Boundary Test: Rapid Dynamic Power Limit Changes
subsection content: This test validates system stability and enforcement of RAPL limits during rapid and dynamic changes to PL1 and PL2 under varying workloads.

title: Stress and Boundary Test: Rapid Dynamic Power Limit Changes
subsection content: With RAPL limits enabled and not locked, apply a pseudo-random workload and rapidly change PL1/PL2 and time windows within legal ranges at 1ms intervals via TPMI or CSR.

title: Stress and Boundary Test: Rapid Dynamic Power Limit Changes
subsection content: Workload pattern and power limit/time window values are varied pseudo-randomly within legal ranges.

title: Stress and Boundary Test: Rapid Dynamic Power Limit Changes
subsection content: Requires RAPL limits enabled and not locked, access to TPMI or CSR, and tools for workload generation and monitoring.

title: Stress and Boundary Test: Rapid Dynamic Power Limit Changes
subsection content: Involves RAPL, PL1/PL2, frequency scaling, and chiplet communication.

title: Stress and Boundary Test: Rapid Dynamic Power Limit Changes
subsection content: Observability via IMON, RAPL energy status, RAPL_perf_status, and frequency scaling parameters.

title: Rapid Dynamic PL1/PL2 Changes Under Varying Workload
subsection content: With RAPL limits enabled and not locked, apply a workload that alternates between idle and maximum utilization in a pseudo-random pattern. Rapidly change PL1 and PL2 power limits and time windows within legal ranges via TPMI or CSR at intervals of 1ms or less. Monitor system power consumption, RAPL energy status, RAPL_perf_status, and frequency scaling parameters sent to chiplet.

title: Rapid Dynamic PL1/PL2 Changes Under Varying Workload
subsection content: 1. Boot system with RAPL limits enabled and not locked. 2. Start pseudo-random workload generator. 3. At 1ms intervals or less, change PL1/PL2 and time windows within legal ranges via TPMI or CSR. 4. Continuously monitor IMON, RAPL energy status, RAPL_perf_status, and frequency scaling parameters. 5. Collect logs for analysis.

title: Rapid Dynamic PL1/PL2 Changes Under Varying Workload
subsection content: Pass if system tracks the most restrictive limit, maintains stability, and enforces limits without exceeding programmed values. Fail if system exceeds limits, becomes unstable, or does not enforce the most restrictive limit.

title: Rapid Dynamic PL1/PL2 Changes Under Varying Workload
subsection content: Use IMON, RAPL energy status, RAPL_perf_status, and frequency scaling logs to confirm correct enforcement and stability.

title: Rapid Dynamic PL1/PL2 Changes Under Varying Workload
subsection content: Enable SST, Central, and Concurrency checkers.

subsection content: Test Requirements: BIOS with RAPL enabled and not locked, default OS and drivers.

subsection content: During rapid dynamic changes to PL1/PL2 and time windows, monitor the frequency scaling parameter sent to chiplet by Primecode every 1ms. Verify that frequency scaling adjusts according to the most restrictive RAPL limit and workload demand.

title: Monitor Frequency Scaling Response to Rapid RAPL Changes
subsection content: 1. Configure system as in previous TC. 2. Apply rapid PL1/PL2 changes and pseudo-random workload. 3. Monitor frequency scaling parameter sent to chiplet. 4. Correlate frequency scaling changes with RAPL limit updates and workload transitions. 5. Collect and analyze logs.

title: Monitor Frequency Scaling Response to Rapid RAPL Changes
subsection content: Pass if frequency scaling parameter adjusts as expected and system remains stable. Fail if frequency scaling does not respond or system is unstable.

title: Monitor Frequency Scaling Response to Rapid RAPL Changes
subsection content: Use frequency scaling logs and workload correlation to confirm correct behavior.

title: Verify RAPL_perf_status and Power Enforcement During Rapid Changes
subsection content: Pass if RAPL_perf_status reflects performance impact only when limits are enforced and system power does not exceed programmed values. Fail if RAPL_perf_status does not reflect enforcement or system exceeds limits.

title: Negative Test: Disable Power Limit and Observe Throttling
subsection content: This test validates that disabling a power limit does not allow system power to exceed platform constraints, as hardware throttling mechanisms should engage.

title: Negative Test: Disable Power Limit and Observe Throttling
subsection content: Disable a power limit via supported interface, apply workload to exceed previous limit, and monitor for hardware throttling and system stability.

title: Negative Test: Disable Power Limit and Observe Throttling
subsection content: No randomization; test disables power limit and applies workload.

title: Negative Test: Disable Power Limit and Observe Throttling
subsection content: Requires RAPL limits enabled and not locked, access to PBM_DISABLE or equivalent, and monitoring tools for power, RAPL_perf_status, and throttle signals.

title: Negative Test: Disable Power Limit and Observe Throttling
subsection content: Involves RAPL, PBM_DISABLE, hardware throttling (PROCHOT, PMAX), and system power management.

title: Negative Test: Disable Power Limit and Observe Throttling
subsection content: Observability via IMON, RAPL_perf_status, IO_THERM_INDICATIONS.FAST_RAPL_THROTTLE[22], PROCHOT, and PMAX.

title: Disable Power Limit and Apply Exceeding Workload
subsection content: With RAPL limits enabled and not locked, disable a power limit (e.g., set PBM_DISABLE) via supported interface. Apply a workload that would exceed the previously set power limit. Monitor system power consumption, RAPL_perf_status, and check for assertion of throttling signals (IO_THERM_INDICATIONS.FAST_RAPL_THROTTLE[22], PROCHOT, PMAX).

title: Disable Power Limit and Apply Exceeding Workload
subsection content: 1. Boot system with RAPL limits enabled and not locked. 2. Disable power limit via PBM_DISABLE or equivalent. 3. Apply workload to exceed previous power limit. 4. Monitor IMON, RAPL_perf_status, IO_THERM_INDICATIONS.FAST_RAPL_THROTTLE[22], PROCHOT, and PMAX. 5. Collect logs for analysis.

title: Disable Power Limit and Apply Exceeding Workload
subsection content: Pass if system throttles via hardware mechanisms when physical constraints are exceeded, RAPL_perf_status does not increment for disabled limit, and system remains stable. Fail if system exceeds constraints, does not throttle, or is unstable.

title: Disable Power Limit and Apply Exceeding Workload
subsection content: Use IMON, RAPL_perf_status, and throttle signal logs to confirm correct hardware response.

title: Disable Power Limit and Apply Exceeding Workload
subsection content: Enable SST, Central, and Concurrency checkers.

title: Disable Power Limit and Apply Exceeding Workload
subsection content: Tool and Content Requirements: OSBV/SVOS, PMON, PythonSV, workload generator.

title: Disable Power Limit and Apply Exceeding Workload
subsection content: Test Requirements: BIOS with RAPL enabled and not locked, default OS and drivers.

title: Disable Power Limit and Apply Exceeding Workload
subsection title: Test HW Requirements and HW Configuration *
subsection content: Platform with supported SoC, power and throttle signal monitoring instrumentation.

title: Monitor Throttle Signals After Power Limit Disable
subsection content: After disabling a power limit, continuously monitor IO_THERM_INDICATIONS.FAST_RAPL_THROTTLE[22], PROCHOT, and PMAX signals during workload execution to verify that hardware throttling is asserted when necessary.

title: Monitor Throttle Signals After Power Limit Disable
subsection content: 1. Configure system as in previous TC. 2. Disable power limit and apply workload. 3. Continuously monitor IO_THERM_INDICATIONS.FAST_RAPL_THROTTLE[22], PROCHOT, and PMAX. 4. Correlate throttle signal assertions with workload and power consumption. 5. Collect and analyze logs.

title: Monitor Throttle Signals After Power Limit Disable
subsection content: Pass if throttle signals are asserted when physical constraints are exceeded and system remains stable. Fail if signals are not asserted or system is unstable.

title: Monitor Throttle Signals After Power Limit Disable
subsection content: Use throttle signal logs and workload correlation to confirm correct hardware throttling.

title: Monitor Throttle Signals After Power Limit Disable
subsection content: Enable SST, Central, and Concurrency checkers.

title: Monitor Throttle Signals After Power Limit Disable
subsection content: Tool and Content Requirements: OSBV/SVOS, PMON, PythonSV, workload generator.

title: Monitor Throttle Signals After Power Limit Disable
subsection content: Test Requirements: BIOS with RAPL enabled and not locked, default OS and drivers.

title: Monitor Throttle Signals After Power Limit Disable
subsection title: Test HW Requirements and HW Configuration *
subsection content: Platform with supported SoC, throttle signal monitoring instrumentation.

title: Verify RAPL_perf_status Does Not Increment for Disabled Limit
subsection content: After disabling a power limit, monitor RAPL_perf_status to ensure it does not increment for the disabled limit during workload execution. Correlate RAPL_perf_status with power limit status and throttle events.

title: Verify RAPL_perf_status Does Not Increment for Disabled Limit
subsection content: 1. Configure system as in previous TCs. 2. Disable power limit and apply workload. 3. Continuously monitor RAPL_perf_status during test. 4. Correlate RAPL_perf_status with power limit status and throttle signal assertions. 5. Collect logs for analysis.

title: Verify RAPL_perf_status Does Not Increment for Disabled Limit
subsection content: Pass if RAPL_perf_status does not increment for disabled limit and system remains stable. Fail if RAPL_perf_status increments or system is unstable.

title: Verify RAPL_perf_status Does Not Increment for Disabled Limit
subsection content: Use RAPL_perf_status and power limit status logs to confirm correct behavior.

title: Verify RAPL_perf_status Does Not Increment for Disabled Limit
subsection content: Enable SST, Central, and Concurrency checkers.

title: Verify RAPL_perf_status Does Not Increment for Disabled Limit
subsection content: Tool and Content Requirements: OSBV/SVOS, PMON, PythonSV, workload generator.

title: Verify RAPL_perf_status Does Not Increment for Disabled Limit
subsection content: Test Requirements: BIOS with RAPL enabled and not locked, default OS and drivers.

title: Verify RAPL_perf_status Does Not Increment for Disabled Limit
subsection title: Test HW Requirements and HW Configuration *
